publication_title: Whole-Blood Transcriptomics Reveals a Systemic Interferon–NF-κB Immune Activation Signature in Uveitis Patients

biological_process_synthesis.text: The DEG profile from whole blood of uveitis patients reveals a systemic immune activation state characterized by strong interferon-driven transcription, NF-κB–mediated inflammatory amplification, and enhanced cytokine receptor signaling. Both innate and adaptive immune pathways are upregulated, suggesting coordinated activation of myeloid cells and T lymphocytes. Enrichment of antigen presentation and chemokine signaling pathways indicates enhanced immune surveillance and leukocyte trafficking capacity. Apoptosis and survival pathways are also altered, consistent with prolonged immune cell activation. Collectively, the transcriptional landscape reflects chronic immune stimulation with a prominent interferon–inflammatory axis likely contributing to ocular tissue inflammation.

statements: Uveitis severity correlates with systemic interferon signature intensity, suggesting a stratifiable IFN-high subtype.
Persistent NF-κB activation in circulating monocytes contributes to ocular tissue infiltration and relapse risk.
Chemokine receptor upregulation (e.g., CCR7/CXCR4 axis) promotes trafficking of autoreactive T cells to the eye.
JAK-STAT pathway hyperactivation predicts responsiveness to JAK inhibitor therapy.
Altered apoptosis regulation prolongs survival of pathogenic immune subsets in circulation.
The DEG pattern suggests uveitis may involve a systemic interferonopathy-like signature rather than solely localized ocular inflammation.
Concurrent enrichment of innate and adaptive modules implies dual-compartment immune dysregulation.
Chemotaxis pathway enrichment indicates circulatory immune priming preceding ocular infiltration.
Apoptosis-survival pathway alterations suggest impaired immune contraction mechanisms contributing to chronicity.

modules.associated_phenotype: Immune cell migration to ocular tissue

summaries.summary: GO enrichment indicates strong activation of interferon signaling, cytokine-mediated pathways, leukocyte activation, and antigen presentation, suggesting systemic immune dysregulation involving both innate and adaptive compartments.

KEGG pathway mapping highlights JAK-STAT signaling, NF-κB signaling, chemokine signaling, and Toll-like receptor pathways, collectively describing a feed-forward inflammatory network consistent with chronic autoimmune activation.

Reactome pathway analysis supports coordinated activation of interferon alpha/beta signaling, cytokine receptor interactions, antigen processing, and immune cell migration, reinforcing a systemic immune activation state with ocular inflammatory consequences.
